--- SOURCE 1: Can 3D printing go mainstream for bridge construction? ---
URL: https://www.newcivilengineer.com/in-depth/can-3d-printing-go-mainstream-for-bridge-construction-25-06-2024/

SUMMARY:
3D printing technology could be deployed for bridge construction, but there are still some factors hindering its adoption.

FULL CONTENT:
![New Civil Engineer](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2019/05/logo1.svg)

# Can 3D printing go mainstream for bridge construction?

25 Jun, 2024

By [Sotiris Kanaris](https://www.newcivilengineer.com/author/sotiris-kanaris/ "Posts by Sotiris Kanaris")

3D printing technology could be deployed for bridge construction, but there are still some factors hindering its adoption.

The evolution of 3D printing technology in the past two decades has made it increasingly popular in the automotive and aerospace industries, as well as in the production of medical devices.

This technology is less common in the construction sector, with most investment directed to housing applications rather than infrastructure.

“There’s much more of a culture for prototyping new technologies in other industries,” says Cowi UK vice president Jo Bonnett, emphasising that testing new technologies on infrastructure projects is especially challenging because of safety parameters.

However, some players in the construction market, particularly in Europe, have decided to explore the use of 3D printing for bridge construction. The 3D printed footbridges that have emerged demonstrate the ability of this technology to produce structures with complex geometries using a range of materials.

### **3D printed bridges**

Dutch contractor Bam Infra Nederland and the Eindhoven University of Technology worked together to develop what they claimed to be the world’s first 3D printed reinforced, prestressed concrete bridge in 2017. The 8m long cycling bridge was made of 3D printed concrete blocks and formed part of a ring road around Gemert in the Netherlands.

Since then, the contractor has deployed this technology in other projects including a 29.5m 3D printed concrete cycling bridge in Nijmegen, as well as three pedestrian bridges in the province of North Holland.

A different type of 3D printed concrete footbridge was unveiled at the Venice Architecture Biennale in Italy in 2021. The “Striatus” was a 16m span arched, unreinforced masonry footbridge with five access points and composed of 3D printed hollow concrete blocks.

![](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2024/06/phoenix-3d-printed-bridge-1024x682.jpg)

The Phoenix unreinforced masonry footbridge was built using 104 3D printed hollow concrete blocks. 40% of the ink used was made of recycled materials. (Photo: Block Research Group)

![](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2024/06/phoenix-3d-printed-bridge-1024x682.jpg)

The Phoenix unreinforced masonry footbridge was built using 104 3D printed hollow concrete blocks. 40% of the ink used was made of recycled materials. (Photo: Block Research Group)

The project was undertaken by the Block Research Group at ETH Zurich university and Zaha Hadid Architects Computation & Design Group in collaboration with additive manufacturing specialists incremental3D and building materials manufacturer Holcim.

Holcim head of global R&D, innovation and intellectual property Edelio Bermejo says there was no need for reinforcement because the principle of Roman arches was followed. The 53 blocks were assembled on top of a formwork in a way that they remained in place after the formwork was removed.

Following the Biennale, the collaboration continued with the aim of creating a more sustainable version of the bridge. The “Phoenix” – which has been standing at Holcim’s Innovation Hub in Lyon, France since the end of last year – was developed using a Holcim customised concrete ink which partially consists of recycled materials – sand from the demolition of Striatus and fully recycled binder.

The biggest advantage of 3D printing is that you can print geometries that you cannot produce easily using traditional manufacturing methods

Apart from concrete, other materials have also been used as ink for 3D printed bridges.

In 2021, a 12m long 3D printed steel bridge across a canal in Amsterdam opened to the public. It was developed by Dutch technology start-up MX3D with designers Joris Laarman Lab, Arup as lead structural engineers and a host of other collaborators.

The bridge has since been removed after the two year permit granted by the city expired.

“It was monitored for the whole time it was in position and didn’t show any signs of degradation,” says Imperial College London department of civil and environmental engineering professor Leroy Gardner.

![](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2024/06/09-Phoenix_copyright-Holcim-web-scaled.jpg)

Phoenix under construction (Photo:Holcim)

![](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2024/06/09-Phoenix_copyright-Holcim-web-scaled.jpg)

Phoenix under construction (Photo:Holcim)

The university was involved in testing and monitoring the structure and continues to research 3D printed steel bridges.

Gardner says that a 4m span footbridge was built in the university laboratory with optimised geometry.

“You can’t physically have much less material,” he says. The bridge passed a 4t load test in May.

“We are definitely continuing to pursue this technology. It is still early days, even though we’ve got this nice example in Amsterdam. There are more grades of steel and more geometries [to explore], and there is a lot more to understand about reliability,” he adds.

Royal HaskoningDHV has been focusing on 3D printed fibreglass reinforced plastic (FRP) bridges since 2018. Its manager of digital engineering and senior bridge consultant Kees van IJselmuijden says: “The reason why we went for FRP printing is the climate. We must have lighter structures, lighter foundations, recycled materials as a basis and demountable and reusable materials.”

He says the company produced and tested several models over the years. In April, the company assembled an 8m long 3D printed footbridge with a load capacity of 10kN/m2 in Gemeente Putten municipality, in the Netherlands. The parapet was also 3D printed.

Van IJselmuijden says that the company is currently looking into 3D printed columns.

### **Benefits**

“The biggest advantage of 3D printing is that you can print geometries that you cannot produce easily using traditional manufacturing methods,” says Gardner.

“The potential advantage of that is that you can print structures that are more optimised, so using less material in a world where using less material is becoming more important for sustainability reasons.”

Bonnett agrees that 3D printing could result in the minimisation of material use through topology optimisation and emphasises the consequent wastage reduction compared to traditional construction methods.

The reduction in material results in carbon savings, but so is the circularity of 3D printed structures according to Bermejo. He says that 40% of the customised concrete ink used to produce the 104 blocks for Phoenix was “circular”.

The recycled material alongside the sourcing of local materials and design optimisation used for Phoenix led to a 25% carbon footprint reduction compared to Striatus.

![](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2024/06/phoenix-3d-printed-bridge-3-web.jpg)

Bridge elements are made up from printed layers (Photo: incremental3D)

![](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2024/06/phoenix-3d-printed-bridge-3-web.jpg)

Bridge elements are made up from printed layers (Photo: incremental3D)

Gardner says another way that 3D printing could help the circular economy is by enabling the reuse of existing elements.

He gives the example of a used beam that does not exactly match the requirements of a project, for example because 10% more load carrying capacity is needed. Gardner explains that the element could be strengthened by using 3D printing to add material.

“I think if it is set up on a good scale, which will take some time and logistics, 3D printing can facilitate wider reuse of material and reuse is miles better environmentally than recycling,” he says.

Gardner says the construction industry has to date under-utilised automation and 3D printing can help it move towards greater automation.

Bermejo adds that with the current shortage of skilled labour, such as masons, the use of this technology means that less labour will be required for bridge construction.

### **Low adoption**

All of this raises a question. With all these benefits identified and tested, why has 3D printing yet to become a mainstream bridge construction method?

“It very much remains a research type application,” says Bonnett.

“Almost all the examples that I’ve come across have an academic involvement because there aren’t published codified guidelines for design. And we’re still learning about the way that the materials will behave once they’ve been printed into place,” says Bonnett.

She believes that the uncertainty about structural performance and the absence of design guidelines are impacting the wider adoption of this technology.

“The safety element and the relative conservatism of our industry are factors as well,” adds Bonnett.

In his article “Metal additive manufacturing in structural engineering – review, advances, opportunities and outlook” published in *Structures* journal in 2023, Gardner wrote that there are still unanswered questions in relation to fundamental mechanical properties; consistency of short term and long term structural performance; geometric accuracy and variability; speed and cost of construction; quality assurance; design; and certification.

![](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2024/06/Picture1.png)

3D printing technology was used to create parapet for a footbridge in the Netherlands (Photo: Royal HaskoningDHV)

![](https://cdn.ca.emap.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2024/06/Picture1.png)

3D printing technology was used to create parapet for a footbridge in the Netherlands (Photo: Royal HaskoningDHV)

The scale of bridge structures means that the printing technology itself has been a limiting factor in the adoption of this method.

Van IJselmuijden says Royal HaskoningDHV initially used the same technology as the automotive industry but it was only able to produce small components.

The technology has evolved considerably since then.

“Now we have a printer that is 12m long, 4m wide and 2.5m high. So now we’re talking about bridge decks that could be 12m long,” he says.

Van IJselmuijden also cites cost as a reason for the dearth of 3D printed bridges. He says the cost is high because of the testing required and materials costs.

With the higher cost, it is important to find clients willing to invest in this technology. Bermejo says “you need a client to be innovative and take some risk” if the adoption of this technology is to increase.

“The bigger clients have the position and the responsibility to be early adopters,” Gardner emphasises.

### **Future use**

“I don’t think we will have a 40m span 3D printed bridge, not for the next 10 to 20 years. I think the first applications will be pedestrian bridges or strengthening current bridges to extend their lifespan,” says van IJselmuijden.

3D printing will complement, rather than replace existing construction methods, according to Gardner.

“I think the future role of 3D printing in bridge construction will be more about where we can mix the different manufacturing methods in order to get the best of all worlds.

The bigger clients have the position and the responsibility to be early adopters

“If you’ve got some more detailed parts, let’s make use of 3D printing to create the intricate geometry.”

Bonnett says initially 3D printing could be used on a smaller scale to manufacture certain bridge components. She points to the 3D printed concrete staircase installed by Bam for the new Sighthill Bridge across the M8 in Glasgow.

She believes that by starting with small components the industry will build confidence in the method to then explore its adoption for larger components or structures.

“I think we have to look at where the biggest gains are to be had from this technology and start there.It would be useful for perhaps clients and designers to come together to work out where the biggest drivers would be to adoption and then to address the barriers in that particular application,” Bonnett says.

“It would be useful to bring together the potential suppliers for knowledge sharing, because at the moment I don’t think there is a particular forum for doing that. This will better enable the transfer of knowledge acquired in other industries into construction.”

*Like what you've read? [To receive New Civil Engineer's daily and weekly newsletters click here.](https://www.newcivilengineer.com/account/newsletter/)*

--- SOURCE 2: 3D Printing Technology Tested on Historic Bridge in Great ... ---
URL: https://3dprinting.com/news/3d-printing-technology-tested-on-historic-bridge-in-great-barrington-as-potential-solution-for-aging-infrastructure/

SUMMARY:
Researchers from UMass Amherst and MIT have successfully applied 3D printing technology to repair a bridge in Great Barrington, Massachusetts.

FULL CONTENT:
![3D Printing](/wp-content/themes/genesis-sample-dev/images/3d-printing-logo.png "3D Printing")
![qidi](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![qidi](/wp-content/uploads/3dprinting1728x444-1.jpg)

# 3D Printing Technology Tested on Historic Bridge in Great Barrington as Potential Solution for Aging Infrastructure

June 16, 2025

Researchers from UMass Amherst and MIT have successfully applied 3D printing technology to repair a bridge in Great Barrington, Massachusetts. The test utilized a technique called cold spray, which involves spraying high-velocity metal powder particles to coat and restore sections of deteriorated beams. This demonstration marks the first known application of cold spray additive manufacturing on a bridge structure.

![3D Printing Technology Tested on Historic Bridge in Great Barrington as Potential Solution for Aging Infrastructure](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20480%20320'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![3D Printing Technology Tested on Historic Bridge in Great Barrington as Potential Solution for Aging Infrastructure](https://3dprinting.com/wp-content/uploads/73587398.jpg)

“Now that we’ve completed this proof-of-concept repair, we see a clear path to a solution that is much faster, less costly, easier, and less invasive,” said Simos Gerasimidis, associate professor of civil and environmental engineering at UMass Amherst. The project involved collaboration with the Massachusetts Department of Transportation, Massachusetts Technology Collaborative, the U.S. Department of Transportation, and the Federal Highway Administration.

The technique could help address America’s aging bridge infrastructure. According to the 2025 Report Card for America’s Infrastructure, 49% of the nation’s bridges are only in “fair” condition and 6.8% are rated “poor,” with projected restoration costs exceeding $191 billion. In Massachusetts alone, about 9% of the state’s 5,295 bridges are structurally deficient.

One significant advantage of the cold spray method is minimal traffic disruption during repairs. The process is complemented by 3D LiDAR scanning that allows precise identification of corrosion areas and targeted material application. “By combining scanning with precise material deposition, we can be very targeted and say, ‘we’re going to print here and here and here and we’re going to give this bridge another 10 years of life,’ which is huge,” Gerasimidis explained.

The red bridge in Great Barrington, formerly known as the “Brown Bridge” and built in 1949, is scheduled for demolition in a few years. After demolition, researchers plan to take the treated beams to their lab for further testing to evaluate the effectiveness of the repair technique. They will measure how successfully the deposited steel powder adhered to the structure and determine its mechanical strength properties.

John Hart, Class of 1922 Professor in the Department of Mechanical Engineering at MIT, noted: “This is a tremendous collaboration where cutting-edge technology is brought to address a critical need for infrastructure in the commonwealth and across the United States. I think we’re just at the beginning of a digital transformation of bridge inspection, repair and maintenance, among many other important use cases.”

Source: [umass.edu](https://www.umass.edu/news/article/can-3d-printing-help-repair-nations-aging-bridges-umass-amherst-researchers-use-great)

--- SOURCE 3: “Cold spray” 3D printing technique proves effective for on-site bridge ... ---
URL: https://news.mit.edu/2025/cold-spray-3d-printing-technique-effective-bridge-repair-0620

SUMMARY:
The technique, called cold spray, can extend the life of beams, reinforcing them with newly deposited steel.

FULL CONTENT:
[Suggestions or feedback?](http://web.mit.edu/feedback)

## [MIT News | Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/)

## Browse By

### Topics

#### Breadcrumb

# “Cold spray” 3D printing technique proves effective for on-site bridge repair

### Press Contact:

![Seven men wearing safety yellow work vests and hardhats pose by a metal I-beam that has a small gray patch in its center.](/themes/mit/src/img/placeholder/placeholder--news-article--image-gallery.jpg)
![A man wearing a MassDOT safety vest and hardhat examines a gray patch on an orange I-beam](/themes/mit/src/img/placeholder/placeholder--news-article--image-gallery.jpg)
![A man adjusts tubing in a rack of large compressed gas tanks.](/themes/mit/src/img/placeholder/placeholder--news-article--image-gallery.jpg)

Previous image
Next image

More than half of the nation’s 623,218 bridges are experiencing significant deterioration. Through an in-field case study conducted in western Massachusetts, a team led by the University of Massachusetts at Amherst in collaboration with researchers from the MIT Department of Mechanical Engineering (MechE) has just successfully demonstrated that 3D printing may provide a cost-effective, minimally disruptive solution.

“Anytime you drive, you go under or over a corroded bridge,” says Simos Gerasimidis, associate professor of civil and environmental engineering at UMass Amherst and former visiting professor in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering at MIT, in a [press release](https://www.umass.edu/news/article/can-3d-printing-help-repair-nations-aging-bridges-umass-amherst-researchers-use-great). “They are everywhere. It’s impossible to avoid, and their condition often shows significant deterioration. We know the numbers.”

The numbers, according to the American Society of Civil Engineers’ [2025 Report Card for America’s Infrastructure](https://infrastructurereportcard.org/), are staggering: Across the United States, 49.1 percent of the nation’s 623,218 bridges are in “fair” condition and 6.8 percent are in “poor” condition. The projected cost to restore all of these failing bridges exceeds $191 billion.

A proof-of-concept repair took place last month on a small, corroded section of a bridge in Great Barrington, Massachusetts. The technique, called cold spray, can extend the life of beams, reinforcing them with newly deposited steel. The process accelerates particles of powdered steel in heated, compressed gas, and then a technician uses an applicator to spray the steel onto the beam. Repeated sprays create multiple layers, restoring thickness and other structural properties.

![Video thumbnail](https://i1.ytimg.com/vi/dXAQ7YDVHOk/maxresdefault.jpg)

This method has proven to be an effective solution for other large structures like submarines, airplanes, and ships, but bridges present a problem on a greater scale. Unlike movable vessels, stationary bridges cannot be brought to the 3D printer — the printer must be brought on-site — and, to lessen systemic impacts, repairs must also be made with minimal disruptions to traffic, which the new approach allows.

“Now that we’ve completed this proof-of-concept repair, we see a clear path to a solution that is much faster, less costly, easier, and less invasive,” says Gerasimidis. “To our knowledge, this is a first. Of course, there is some R&D that needs to be developed, but this is a huge milestone to that.”

“This is a tremendous collaboration where cutting-edge technology is brought to address a critical need for infrastructure in the commonwealth and across the United States,” says John Hart, Class of 1922 Professor and head of the Department of MechE at MIT. Hart and Haden Quinlan, senior program manager in the Center for Advanced Production Technologies at MIT, are leading MIT’s efforts in in the project. Hart is also faculty co-lead of the recently announced [MIT Initiative for New Manufacturing](https://inm.mit.edu/).

“Integrating digital systems with advanced physical processing is the future of infrastructure,” says Quinlan. “We’re excited to have moved this technology beyond the lab and into the field, and grateful to our collaborators in making this work possible.”

UMass says the Massachusetts Department of Transportation (MassDOT) has been a valued research partner, helping to identify the problem and providing essential support for the development and demonstration of the technology. Technical guidance and funding support were provided by the MassDOT Highway Division and the Research and Technology Transfer Program.

Equipment for this project was supported through the Massachusetts Manufacturing Innovation Initiative, a statewide program led by the Massachusetts Technology Collaborative (MassTech)’s Center for Advanced Manufacturing that helps bridge the gap between innovation and commercialization in hard tech manufacturing.

“It’s a very Massachusetts success story,” Gerasimidis says. “It involves MassDOT being open-minded to new ideas. It involves UMass and MIT putting [together] the brains to do it. It involves MassTech to bring manufacturing back to Massachusetts. So, I think it’s a win-win for everyone involved here.”

The bridge in Great Barrington is scheduled for demolition in a few years. After demolition occurs, the recently-sprayed beams will be taken back to UMass for testing and measurement to study how well the deposited steel powder adhered to the structure in the field compared to in a controlled lab setting, if it corroded further after it was sprayed, and determine its mechanical properties.

This demonstration builds on several years of research by the UMass and MIT teams, including development of a “digital thread” approach to scan corroded beam surfaces and determine material deposition profiles, alongside laboratory studies of cold spray and other additive manufacturing approaches that are suited to field deployment.

Altogether, this work is a collaborative effort among UMass Amherst, MIT MechE, MassDOT, the Massachusetts Technology Collaborative (MassTech), the U.S. Department of Transportation, and the Federal Highway Administration. Research reports are available on [the MassDOT website](https://www.mass.gov/doc/feasibility-of-3d-printing-applications-for-highway-infrastructure-construction-and-maintenance-final-report/download).

--- SOURCE 5: 3D Printed Bridge - IAAC Barcelona ---
URL: https://iaac.net/projects/3d-printed-bridge-development/

SUMMARY:
The 3D Printed Bridge stands as a milestone in architectural and civil engineering innovation. It was created through large-scale 3D printing techniques.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://px.ads.linkedin.com/collect/?pid=8572121&fmt=gif)

# 3D Printed Bridge

![IAAC Barcelona]()

## A collaboration between IAAC and ACCIONA setting a new standard in sustainable construction, integrating advanced technology and environmental consciousness into the urban landscape.

# IAAC 3D Printed Bridge

## The Challenge

The construction sector faces the ongoing challenge of minimising material waste while maximising structural efficiency and exploring new technologies to enhance sustainability. Traditional building methods often limit the creative potential of architectural design, especially in public infrastructure projects. Integrating advanced technologies such as 3D printing into civil engineering remains an underexplored avenue that holds promise for revolutionising construction techniques.

## Our Solution

A citizen-driven approach that showcases the potential of having local makers be key suppliers of European cities’ goods. The project works to leverage heritage as a catalyIAAC designed and realised the world’s first 3D-printed pedestrian bridge, located in the urban park of Castilla-La Mancha in Alcobendas, Madrid. This footbridge, measuring 12 metres in length and 1.75 metres in width, was constructed using micro-reinforced concrete. The design employed parametric design tools, allowing for optimal material distribution, reduced waste, and full integration into the public spacest for innovation and social inclusion.

Public infrastructure and innovation in civil engineering. The bridge showcases the potential of large-scale 3D printing technologies, transforming traditional construction practices.

The project utilised parametric design, enabling a more efficient material distribution and structural optimisation. The use of generative algorithms permitted complex forms to emerge naturally, reflecting the fluidity and efficiency of structures found in nature. This approach allowed the creation of a lightweight yet structurally sound bridge with reduced material use and minimise waste through the recycling of raw materials during production.

![IAAC Barcelona]()

### The Project

The 3D Printed Bridge stands as a milestone in architectural and civil engineering innovation. It was created through large-scale 3D printing techniques and designed using computational tools to create a structure that maximises material efficiency while maintaining creative freedom. The design mimics the natural complexities of organic forms, and the use of generative algorithms ensures structural optimisation while maintaining the bridge’s porosity and aesthetic appeal. The project also addresses legislative and practical concerns for implementation in public spaces, creating a functional, safe, and visually striking pedestrian bridge for the citizens of Alcobendas.

### Our Contribution

The IAAC played a central role in the architectural design of the bridge, leveraging more than 15 years of research in large-scale 3D printing. IAAC’s expertise in parametric and computational design allowed for the creation of an innovative structure that pushes the boundaries of civil engineering and architecture. By designing the first 3D-printed footbridge, IAAC has demonstrated the feasibility of large-scale 3D printing in public infrastructure, contributing to a more sustainable, material-efficient future for construction.

### Who is it for?

This project serves the residents of Alcobendas and the general public, providing a sustainable, innovative piece of urban infrastructure. It also aims to inspire architects, engineers, and construction professionals to explore and adopt 3D printing technology for larger, more ambitious projects. Additionally, the project is a testament to the potential of collaborative public and private partnerships in achieving groundbreaking innovation in civil engineering.

enriched_points.expanded_content: 3D printing revolutionizes bridge construction with customizable designs, enhances sustainability through reduced material waste, and allows for innovative shapes not possible with traditional methods.
Using 3D printing, road construction can achieve faster project completion, enhanced durability, and more efficient material usage, which minimizes environmental impact and lowers costs.

3D printing enables the rapid construction of public facilities, offering unique architectural designs and adaptive structures that meet community needs while optimizing resource consumption and reducing timelines.
